summary: Gruber issues a formal public challenge to security researchers David Maynor and Jon Ellch, who claimed to have discovered a Wi-Fi vulnerability in MacBook's built-in AirPort card. He offers to buy a brand-new MacBook and let them keep it if they can remotely hijack it using only its default configuration. The challenge comes with detailed stipulations ensuring the test is fair and uses only out-of-the-box settings. Gruber frames this as a poker-style bet: he expects to be ignored because he believes the researchers demonstrated a class of potential exploit on a rigged machine rather than a real vulnerability. He argues the $1,099 cost is worth it either to confirm a genuine security risk or to call out what he suspects is inflated publicity at Mac security's expense.
key_points: Gruber believes Maynor and Ellch demonstrated a theoretical class of Wi-Fi exploit on a modified MacBook, not a real vulnerability in default hardware
The challenge's 14 stipulations are carefully designed to test only the stock MacBook configuration with no user intervention or rigged setup
Gruber frames the bet using poker logic: the knowledge gained is worth more than the money risked
The challenge is structured so the researchers would only accept if they can actually win, making non-acceptance itself informative
Gruber argues someone needs to hold security researchers accountable when they generate publicity at the expense of Mac's security reputation without proof
money_quotes: If you can hijack a brand-new MacBook out of the box, it's yours to keep.
The idea is that knowledge I'll gain from seeing my opponent's cards is worth more than the bet I expect to lose.
I don't expect to lose this particular bet — but I don't expect to win it, either. I expect to be ignored.
If I'm wrong, it'd be worth $1099 to know that MacBook users are in fact at risk.
If I'm right, someone needs to call Maynor and Ellch on their bullshit.

I think what they've discovered — if they've in fact discovered anything useful at all — is a class of potential Wi-Fi-based exploit, which they demonstrated on a rigged MacBook to generate publicity at the expense of the Mac's renowned reputation for security.
key_insight: When security researchers make dramatic claims but refuse to demonstrate them under fair conditions, a public challenge with real stakes is the most effective way to separate genuine vulnerabilities from publicity stunts.
